title: Strange Attractor: The Definitive Biography of Terence McKenna
author: Ferdinando Buscema

full_text:
If you already know who Terence McKenna was, no selling is necessary. If you don't, this is the place to start. Though if you're reading Boing Boing, chances are you already do.

Twenty-five years after his passing, we finally have what can only be called the definitive biography: the man, the character, the ideas, the contradictions, and the enduring legacy. Graham St John's Strange Attractor: The Hallucinatory Life of Terence McKenna (MIT Press, 2025) charts the full arc: from McKenna's early explorations, inner and outer, through his ascent to global countercultural fame, to his untimely death in 2000 and the enormous outpouring of love that followed. It's a treasure trove for hardcore McKennites and a luxurious introduction for those who have only encountered his name in passing.

Terence McKenna

McKenna was a man of many latitudes: scholar of the uncanny, edge runner, stand-up philosopher, psycho-activist, psychedelic saint. Darling of the psytrance scene and in high demand on festival stages worldwide. Éminence grise of the weird. Magellan of the imagination. Above all, a true magister ludi of language whose singular voice could hold a room spellbound.

The range of ideas he sampled and remixed was staggering: psychedelic states and substances, of course, but also alchemy, Gnosticism, Hermeticism, media theory, alien languages, space travel, and the architecture of time and history itself. No stone left unturned for this boundary hacker. Some of his prophecies proved uncannily prescient; others were, let's say, optimistically wrong. Along with legions of admirers, he also attracted serious skeptics. His famous Stoned Ape Hypothesis—proposing that psilocybin mushrooms catalyzed the evolution of human language and consciousness—drew both fascination and withering scientific criticism. Yet his speculations ignited generations of inner explorers, and his books, lectures, articles, and legendary "raps" remain very much alive.

Strange Attractor

St John's biography is rigorous in its scholarship yet reads like a novel—propulsive, captivating, occasionally jaw-dropping. It unfolds in two movements: first a chronological account of McKenna's life and adventures, then a thematic dive into his ideas and their reverberations. The result rewards both newcomers and the already converted. I've been reading McKenna for thirty years; I still dog-eared half the pages of Strange Attractor. There is always more to discover. Drawing on unpublished materials, journals, letters, and interviews with friends, family, and fellow travellers, St John reconstructs a vivid portrait of the man and his milieu.

Graham St John is a cultural anthropologist, Senior Research Fellow at the University of Huddersfield, and the author of several books on electronic music, festivals, and transformative culture. The foreword is by Erik Davis, a good friend of McKenna and one of the sharpest contemporary scholars of the weird.

The Felt Presence of Terence

Twenty-five years on, McKenna's spirit is still very much present—alive in YouTube videos, endlessly quotable memes, transcripts, soundbites, and raps scattered across the internet. In true Gnostic form, McKenna has migrated into the virtual domain, a disembodied voice that refuses to dissipate. His message still resonates: the primacy of imagination, the importance of direct experience, the stubborn insistence that reality is far stranger and more malleable than consensus culture admits.

In the end, "It's all as real as you can imagine."

Strange Attractor: The Hallucinatory Life of Terence McKenna is the book his legacy deserved.
